The principal deity is {{ill|sahasrabhuja|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|fr|sahasrabhuja=sahasrabhuja|lt=Thousand-Armed Avalokiteshvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sahasrabhuja|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.

== Overview ==
Unpenji is the 66th temple on the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]], located in Tokushima Prefecture (formerly Awa Province), although it is treated as a temple of [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]] for the pilgrimage. It is known as the "Nehan Stage" (涅槃の道場), marking the start of the final section of the pilgrimage. Situated at an elevation of approximately 900 m near the main hall, it is the highest temple on the Shikoku Pilgrimage route and serves as a final checkpoint for pilgrims. The temple is renowned for its hydrangeas in summer, its autumn foliage, and its snow-covered landscapes in winter, made accessible by a ropeway.

== History ==
According to temple tradition, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi) established Unpenji in 789 (Enryaku 8) after ascending Unpenji Mountain to procure timber for the construction of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsūji]]. Deeming the site sacred, he constructed a temple and later conducted esoteric rites, including the secret abhiṣeka, in 807 (Daidō 2). By imperial command from Emperor Saga, he carved the principal deity in 818 (Kōnin 9) and conducted memorial services for the Seven Buddhas. The temple became a training ground for monks and was later designated as an imperial prayer temple by Emperor Seiwa during the Jōgan era (857–877).

In 1098 (Shōtoku 2), the temple was destroyed by fire. A hunter named Yonari, who entered the area chasing deer, was said to have witnessed the manifestation of Kannon and rebuilt the temple out of devotion. During the late Heian period, Gansai Shōnin, considered the temple's restorer, created the existing statues of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and Bishamonten. The temple thrived under the patronage of Sasaki Tsunetaka (Kyōren) during the Kamakura period, who established a seven-hall complex. In 1311 (Ōchō 1), the Saionji family in Kyoto donated temple lands, and in 1363 (Jōji 2), the Ashikaga clan requested the chanting of the Lotus Sutra.

In 1577 (Tenshō 5), the Sengoku daimyō Chōsokabe Motochika visited Unpenji and shared his vision of uniting Shikoku with the temple's head priest.

== Highlights ==
The temple precincts feature several notable structures:
* Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== Cultural Properties ==
; Important Cultural Properties
* Wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon - Attributed to Keijin, carved during the late Heian period. Designated on August 9, 1911.
* Wooden statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten - Created by Keison in 1184 (Juei 3). Designated on October 31, 2018.

== Access ==
* Train: [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]] Dosan Line – 10.5 km from Minawa Station
* Ropeway: 10-minute walk from the mountaintop station
* Parking: Free parking available at the base station; fees a11ly near the temple precincts.

'''Unpenji''' (雲辺寺) is a {{ill|Shingon Omuro sect|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro sect|1=Omuro school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q420739}} temple located near the summit of {{ill|Unpenji Mountain|ja|雲辺寺山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unpenji Mountain|1=Mount Unpenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11660089}} (927 m above sea level) in [[:en:Miyoshi, Tokushima|Miyoshi, Tokushima]], formerly [[:en:Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District, Tokushima]], in Tokushima Prefecture, Japan. The temple is formally known as Kyogozan Senjuin (巨鼇山千手院) and is the 66th temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. The principal deity is {{ill|sahasrabhuja|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|fr|sahasrabhuja=sahasrabhuja|lt=Thousand-Armed Avalokiteshvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sahasrabhuja|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.

== Overview ==
Unpenji is the 66th temple on the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]], located in Tokushima Prefecture (formerly Awa Province), although it is treated as a temple of [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]] for the pilgrimage. It is known as the "Nehan Stage" (涅槃の道場), marking the start of the final section of the pilgrimage. Situated at an elevation of approximately 900 m near the main hall, it is the highest temple on the Shikoku Pilgrimage route and serves as a final checkpoint for pilgrims. The temple is renowned for its hydrangeas in summer, its autumn foliage, and its snow-covered landscapes in winter, made accessible by a ropeway.

== History ==
According to temple tradition, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi) established Unpenji in 789 (Enryaku 8) after ascending Unpenji Mountain to procure timber for the construction of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsūji]]. Deeming the site sacred, he constructed a temple and later conducted esoteric rites, including the secret abhiṣeka, in 807 (Daidō 2). By imperial command from Emperor Saga, he carved the principal deity in 818 (Kōnin 9) and conducted memorial services for the Seven Buddhas. The temple became a training ground for monks and was later designated as an imperial prayer temple by Emperor Seiwa during the Jōgan era (857–877).

In 1098 (Shōtoku 2), the temple was destroyed by fire. A hunter named Yonari, who entered the area chasing deer, was said to have witnessed the manifestation of Kannon and rebuilt the temple out of devotion. During the late Heian period, Gansai Shōnin, considered the temple's restorer, created the existing statues of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and Bishamonten. The temple thrived under the patronage of Sasaki Tsunetaka (Kyōren) during the Kamakura period, who established a seven-hall complex. In 1311 (Ōchō 1), the Saionji family in Kyoto donated temple lands, and in 1363 (Jōji 2), the Ashikaga clan requested the chanting of the Lotus Sutra.

In 1577 (Tenshō 5), the Sengoku daimyō Chōsokabe Motochika visited Unpenji and shared his vision of uniting Shikoku with the temple's head priest.

== Highlights ==
The temple precincts feature several notable structures:
* Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== Cultural Properties ==
; Important Cultural Properties
* Wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon - Attributed to Keijin, carved during the late Heian period. Designated on August 9, 1911.
* Wooden statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten - Created by Keison in 1184 (Juei 3). Designated on October 31, 2018.

== Access ==
* Train: [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]] Dosan Line – 10.5 km from Minawa Station
* Ropeway: 10-minute walk from the mountaintop station
* Parking: Free parking available at the base station; fees a11ly near the temple precincts.

'''Unpenji''' (雲辺寺) is a {{ill|Shingon Omuro sect|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro sect|1=Omuro school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q420739}} temple located near the summit of {{ill|Unpenji Mountain|ja|雲辺寺山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unpenji Mountain|1=Mount Unpenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11660089}} (927 m above sea level) in [[:en:Miyoshi, Tokushima|Miyoshi, Tokushima]], formerly [[:en:Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District, Tokushima]], in Tokushima Prefecture, Japan. The temple is formally known as Kyogozan Senjuin (巨鼇山千手院) and is the 66th temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. The principal deity is {{ill|sahasrabhuja|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|fr|sahasrabhuja=sahasrabhuja|lt=Thousand-Armed Avalokiteshvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sahasrabhuja|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.

== Overview ==
Unpenji is the 66th temple on the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]], located in Tokushima Prefecture (formerly Awa Province), although it is treated as a temple of [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]] for the pilgrimage. It is known as the "Nehan Stage" (涅槃の道場), marking the start of the final section of the pilgrimage. Situated at an elevation of approximately 900 m near the main hall, it is the highest temple on the Shikoku Pilgrimage route and serves as a final checkpoint for pilgrims. The temple is renowned for its hydrangeas in summer, its autumn foliage, and its snow-covered landscapes in winter, made accessible by a ropeway.

== History ==
According to temple tradition, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi) established Unpenji in 789 (Enryaku 8) after ascending Unpenji Mountain to procure timber for the construction of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsūji]]. Deeming the site sacred, he constructed a temple and later conducted esoteric rites, including the secret abhiṣeka, in 807 (Daidō 2). By imperial command from Emperor Saga, he carved the principal deity in 818 (Kōnin 9) and conducted memorial services for the Seven Buddhas. The temple became a training ground for monks and was later designated as an imperial prayer temple by Emperor Seiwa during the Jōgan era (857–877).

In 1098 (Shōtoku 2), the temple was destroyed by fire. A hunter named Yonari, who entered the area chasing deer, was said to have witnessed the manifestation of Kannon and rebuilt the temple out of devotion. During the late Heian period, Gansai Shōnin, considered the temple's restorer, created the existing statues of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and Bishamonten. The temple thrived under the patronage of Sasaki Tsunetaka (Kyōren) during the Kamakura period, who established a seven-hall complex. In 1311 (Ōchō 1), the Saionji family in Kyoto donated temple lands, and in 1363 (Jōji 2), the Ashikaga clan requested the chanting of the Lotus Sutra.

In 1577 (Tenshō 5), the Sengoku daimyō Chōsokabe Motochika visited Unpenji and shared his vision of uniting Shikoku with the temple's head priest.

== Highlights ==
The temple precincts feature several notable structures:
* Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== Cultural Properties ==
; Important Cultural Properties
* Wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon - Attributed to Keijin, carved during the late Heian period. Designated on August 9, 1911.
* Wooden statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten - Created by Keison in 1184 (Juei 3). Designated on October 31, 2018.

== Access ==
* Train: [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]] Dosan Line – 10.5 km from Minawa Station
* Ropeway: 10-minute walk from the mountaintop station
* Parking: Free parking available at the base station; fees a11ly near the temple precincts.

'''Unpenji''' (雲辺寺) is a {{ill|Shingon Omuro sect|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro sect|1=Omuro school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q420739}} temple located near the summit of {{ill|Unpenji Mountain|ja|雲辺寺山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unpenji Mountain|1=Mount Unpenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11660089}} (927 m above sea level) in [[:en:Miyoshi, Tokushima|Miyoshi, Tokushima]], formerly [[:en:Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District, Tokushima]], in Tokushima Prefecture, Japan. The temple is formally known as Kyogozan Senjuin (巨鼇山千手院) and is the 66th temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. The principal deity is {{ill|sahasrabhuja|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|fr|sahasrabhuja=sahasrabhuja|lt=Thousand-Armed Avalokiteshvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sahasrabhuja|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.

== Overview ==
Unpenji is the 66th temple on the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]], located in Tokushima Prefecture (formerly Awa Province), although it is treated as a temple of [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]] for the pilgrimage. It is known as the "Nehan Stage" (涅槃の道場), marking the start of the final section of the pilgrimage. Situated at an elevation of approximately 900 m near the main hall, it is the highest temple on the Shikoku Pilgrimage route and serves as a final checkpoint for pilgrims. The temple is renowned for its hydrangeas in summer, its autumn foliage, and its snow-covered landscapes in winter, made accessible by a ropeway.

== History ==
According to temple tradition, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi) established Unpenji in 789 (Enryaku 8) after ascending Unpenji Mountain to procure timber for the construction of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsūji]]. Deeming the site sacred, he constructed a temple and later conducted esoteric rites, including the secret abhiṣeka, in 807 (Daidō 2). By imperial command from Emperor Saga, he carved the principal deity in 818 (Kōnin 9) and conducted memorial services for the Seven Buddhas. The temple became a training ground for monks and was later designated as an imperial prayer temple by Emperor Seiwa during the Jōgan era (857–877).

In 1098 (Shōtoku 2), the temple was destroyed by fire. A hunter named Yonari, who entered the area chasing deer, was said to have witnessed the manifestation of Kannon and rebuilt the temple out of devotion. During the late Heian period, Gansai Shōnin, considered the temple's restorer, created the existing statues of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and Bishamonten. The temple thrived under the patronage of Sasaki Tsunetaka (Kyōren) during the Kamakura period, who established a seven-hall complex. In 1311 (Ōchō 1), the Saionji family in Kyoto donated temple lands, and in 1363 (Jōji 2), the Ashikaga clan requested the chanting of the Lotus Sutra.

In 1577 (Tenshō 5), the Sengoku daimyō Chōsokabe Motochika visited Unpenji and shared his vision of uniting Shikoku with the temple's head priest.

== Highlights ==
The temple precincts feature several notable structures:
* Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== Cultural Properties ==
; Important Cultural Properties
* Wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon - Attributed to Keijin, carved during the late Heian period. Designated on August 9, 1911.
* Wooden statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten - Created by Keison in 1184 (Juei 3). Designated on October 31, 2018.

== Access ==
* Train: [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]] Dosan Line – 10.5 km from Minawa Station
* Ropeway: 10-minute walk from the mountaintop station
* Parking: Free parking available at the base station; fees a11ly near the temple precincts.

'''Unpenji''' (雲辺寺) is a {{ill|Shingon Omuro sect|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro sect|1=Omuro school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q420739}} temple located near the summit of {{ill|Unpenji Mountain|ja|雲辺寺山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unpenji Mountain|1=Mount Unpenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11660089}} (927 m above sea level) in [[:en:Miyoshi, Tokushima|Miyoshi, Tokushima]], formerly [[:en:Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District, Tokushima]], in Tokushima Prefecture, Japan. The temple is formally known as Kyogozan Senjuin (巨鼇山千手院) and is the 66th temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. The principal deity is {{ill|sahasrabhuja|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|fr|sahasrabhuja=sahasrabhuja|lt=Thousand-Armed Avalokiteshvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sahasrabhuja|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.

== Overview ==
Unpenji is the 66th temple on the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]], located in Tokushima Prefecture (formerly Awa Province), although it is treated as a temple of [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]] for the pilgrimage. It is known as the "Nehan Stage" (涅槃の道場), marking the start of the final section of the pilgrimage. Situated at an elevation of approximately 900 m near the main hall, it is the highest temple on the Shikoku Pilgrimage route and serves as a final checkpoint for pilgrims. The temple is renowned for its hydrangeas in summer, its autumn foliage, and its snow-covered landscapes in winter, made accessible by a ropeway.

== History ==
According to temple tradition, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi) established Unpenji in 789 (Enryaku 8) after ascending Unpenji Mountain to procure timber for the construction of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsūji]]. Deeming the site sacred, he constructed a temple and later conducted esoteric rites, including the secret abhiṣeka, in 807 (Daidō 2). By imperial command from Emperor Saga, he carved the principal deity in 818 (Kōnin 9) and conducted memorial services for the Seven Buddhas. The temple became a training ground for monks and was later designated as an imperial prayer temple by Emperor Seiwa during the Jōgan era (857–877).

In 1098 (Shōtoku 2), the temple was destroyed by fire. A hunter named Yonari, who entered the area chasing deer, was said to have witnessed the manifestation of Kannon and rebuilt the temple out of devotion. During the late Heian period, Gansai Shōnin, considered the temple's restorer, created the existing statues of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and Bishamonten. The temple thrived under the patronage of Sasaki Tsunetaka (Kyōren) during the Kamakura period, who established a seven-hall complex. In 1311 (Ōchō 1), the Saionji family in Kyoto donated temple lands, and in 1363 (Jōji 2), the Ashikaga clan requested the chanting of the Lotus Sutra.

In 1577 (Tenshō 5), the Sengoku daimyō Chōsokabe Motochika visited Unpenji and shared his vision of uniting Shikoku with the temple's head priest.

== Highlights ==
The temple precincts feature several notable structures:
* Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== Cultural Properties ==
; Important Cultural Properties
* Wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon - Attributed to Keijin, carved during the late Heian period. Designated on August 9, 1911.
* Wooden statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten - Created by Keison in 1184 (Juei 3). Designated on October 31, 2018.

== Access ==
* Train: [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]] Dosan Line – 10.5 km from Minawa Station
* Ropeway: 10-minute walk from the mountaintop station
* Parking: Free parking available at the base station; fees a11ly near the temple precincts.

In 1098 (Shōtoku 2), the temple was destroyed by fire. During the late Heian period, Gansai Shōnin, considered the temple's restorer, created the existing statues of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and Bishamonten. The temple thrived under the patronage of Sasaki Tsunetaka (Kyōren) during the Kamakura period, who established a seven-hall complex. In 1311 (Ōchō 1), the Saionji family in Kyoto donated temple lands, and in 1363 (Jōji 2), the Ashikaga clan requested the chanting of the Lotus Sutra.

In 1577 (Tenshō 5), the Sengoku daimyō Chōsokabe Motochika visited Unpenji and shared his vision of uniting Shikoku with the temple's head priest.

== Highlights ==
The temple precincts feature several notable structures:
* Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== Cultural Properties ==
; Important Cultural Properties
* Wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon - Attributed to Keijin, carved during the late Heian period. Designated on August 9, 1911.
* Wooden statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten - Created by Keison in 1184 (Juei 3). Designated on October 31, 2018.

== Access ==
* Train: [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]] Dosan Line – 10.5 km from Minawa Station
* Ropeway: 10-minute walk from the mountaintop station
* Parking: Free parking available at the base station; fees a11ly near the temple precincts.

'''Unpenji''' (雲辺寺) is a {{ill|Shingon Omuro sect|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro sect|1=Omuro school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q420739}} temple located near the summit of {{ill|Unpenji Mountain|ja|雲辺寺山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unpenji Mountain|1=Mount Unpenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11660089}} (927 m above sea level) in [[:en:Miyoshi, Tokushima|Miyoshi, Tokushima]], formerly [[:en:Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District, Tokushima]], in Tokushima Prefecture, Japan. The temple is formally known as Kyogozan Senjuin (巨鼇山千手院) and is the 66th temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. The principal deity is {{ill|sahasrabhuja|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|fr|sahasrabhuja=sahasrabhuja|lt=Thousand-Armed Avalokiteshvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sahasrabhuja|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.

== Overview ==
Unpenji is the 66th temple on the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]], located in Tokushima Prefecture (formerly Awa Province), although it is treated as a temple of [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]] for the pilgrimage. It is known as the "Nehan Stage" (涅槃の道場), marking the start of the final section of the pilgrimage. Situated at an elevation of approximately 900 m near the main hall, it is the highest temple on the Shikoku Pilgrimage route and serves as a final checkpoint for pilgrims. The temple is renowned for its hydrangeas in summer, its autumn foliage, and its snow-covered landscapes in winter, made accessible by a ropeway.

== History ==
According to temple tradition, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi) established Unpenji in 789 (Enryaku 8) after ascending Unpenji Mountain to procure timber for the construction of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsūji]]. Deeming the site sacred, he constructed a temple and later conducted esoteric rites, including the secret abhiṣeka, in 807 (Daidō 2). By imperial command from Emperor Saga, he carved the principal deity in 818 (Kōnin 9) and conducted memorial services for the Seven Buddhas. The temple became a training ground for monks and was later designated as an imperial prayer temple by Emperor Seiwa during the Jōgan era (857–877).

In 1098 (Shōtoku 2), the temple was destroyed by fire. A hunter named Yonari, who entered the area chasing deer, was said to have witnessed the manifestation of Kannon and rebuilt the temple out of devotion. During the late Heian period, Gansai Shōnin, considered the temple's restorer, created the existing statues of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and Bishamonten. The temple thrived under the patronage of Sasaki Tsunetaka (Kyōren) during the Kamakura period, who established a seven-hall complex. In 1311 (Ōchō 1), the Saionji family in Kyoto donated temple lands, and in 1363 (Jōji 2), the Ashikaga clan requested the chanting of the Lotus Sutra.

In 1577 (Tenshō 5), the Sengoku daimyō Chōsokabe Motochika visited Unpenji and shared his vision of uniting Shikoku with the temple's head priest.

== Highlights ==
The temple precincts feature several notable structures:
* Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== Cultural Properties ==
; Important Cultural Properties
* Wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon - Attributed to Keijin, carved during the late Heian period. Designated on August 9, 1911.
* Wooden statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten - Created by Keison in 1184 (Juei 3). Designated on October 31, 2018.

== Access ==
* Train: [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]] Dosan Line – 10.5 km from Minawa Station
* Ropeway: 10-minute walk from the mountaintop station
* Parking: Free parking available at the base station; fees a11ly near the temple precincts.

'''Unpenji''' (雲辺寺) is a {{ill|Shingon Omuro sect|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro sect|1=Omuro school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q420739}} temple located near the summit of {{ill|Unpenji Mountain|ja|雲辺寺山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unpenji Mountain|1=Mount Unpenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11660089}} (927 m above sea level) in [[:en:Miyoshi, Tokushima|Miyoshi, Tokushima]], formerly [[:en:Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District, Tokushima]], in Tokushima Prefecture, Japan. The temple is formally known as Kyogozan Senjuin (巨鼇山千手院) and is the 66th temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. The principal deity is {{ill|sahasrabhuja|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|fr|sahasrabhuja=sahasrabhuja|lt=Thousand-Armed Avalokiteshvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sahasrabhuja|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.

== Overview ==
Unpenji is the 66th temple on the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]], located in Tokushima Prefecture (formerly Awa Province), although it is treated as a temple of [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]] for the pilgrimage. It is known as the "Nehan Stage" (涅槃の道場), marking the start of the final section of the pilgrimage. Situated at an elevation of approximately 900 m near the main hall, it is the highest temple on the Shikoku Pilgrimage route and serves as a final checkpoint for pilgrims. The temple is renowned for its hydrangeas in summer, its autumn foliage, and its snow-covered landscapes in winter, made accessible by a ropeway.

== History ==
According to temple tradition, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi) established Unpenji in 789 (Enryaku 8) after ascending Unpenji Mountain to procure timber for the construction of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsūji]]. Deeming the site sacred, he constructed a temple and later conducted esoteric rites, including the secret abhiṣeka, in 807 (Daidō 2). By imperial command from Emperor Saga, he carved the principal deity in 818 (Kōnin 9) and conducted memorial services for the Seven Buddhas. The temple became a training ground for monks and was later designated as an imperial prayer temple by Emperor Seiwa during the Jōgan era (857–877).

In 1098 (Shōtoku 2), the temple was destroyed by fire. A hunter named Yonari, who entered the area chasing deer, was said to have witnessed the manifestation of Kannon and rebuilt the temple out of devotion. During the late Heian period, Gansai Shōnin, considered the temple's restorer, created the existing statues of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and Bishamonten. The temple thrived under the patronage of Sasaki Tsunetaka (Kyōren) during the Kamakura period, who established a seven-hall complex. In 1311 (Ōchō 1), the Saionji family in Kyoto donated temple lands, and in 1363 (Jōji 2), the Ashikaga clan requested the chanting of the Lotus Sutra.

In 1577 (Tenshō 5), the Sengoku daimyō Chōsokabe Motochika visited Unpenji and shared his vision of uniting Shikoku with the temple's head priest.

== Highlights ==
The temple precincts feature several notable structures:
* Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== Cultural Properties ==
; Important Cultural Properties
* Wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon - Attributed to Keijin, carved during the late Heian period. Designated on August 9, 1911.
* Wooden statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten - Created by Keison in 1184 (Juei 3). Designated on October 31, 2018.

== Access ==
* Train: [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]] Dosan Line – 10.5 km from Minawa Station
* Ropeway: 10-minute walk from the mountaintop station
* Parking: Free parking available at the base station; fees a11ly near the temple precincts.

'''Unpenji''' (雲辺寺) is a {{ill|Shingon Omuro sect|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro sect|1=Omuro school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q420739}} temple located near the summit of {{ill|Unpenji Mountain|ja|雲辺寺山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unpenji Mountain|1=Mount Unpenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11660089}} (927 m above sea level) in [[:en:Miyoshi, Tokushima|Miyoshi, Tokushima]], formerly [[:en:Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District, Tokushima]], in Tokushima Prefecture, Japan. The temple is formally known as Kyogozan Senjuin (巨鼇山千手院) and is the 66th temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. The principal deity is {{ill|sahasrabhuja|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|fr|sahasrabhuja=sahasrabhuja|lt=Thousand-Armed Avalokiteshvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sahasrabhuja|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.

== Overview ==
Unpenji is the 66th temple on the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]], located in Tokushima Prefecture (formerly Awa Province), although it is treated as a temple of [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]] for the pilgrimage. It is known as the "Nehan Stage" (涅槃の道場), marking the start of the final section of the pilgrimage. Situated at an elevation of approximately 900 m near the main hall, it is the highest temple on the Shikoku Pilgrimage route and serves as a final checkpoint for pilgrims. The temple is renowned for its hydrangeas in summer, its autumn foliage, and its snow-covered landscapes in winter, made accessible by a ropeway.

== History ==
According to temple tradition, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi) established Unpenji in 789 (Enryaku 8) after ascending Unpenji Mountain to procure timber for the construction of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsūji]]. Deeming the site sacred, he constructed a temple and later conducted esoteric rites, including the secret abhiṣeka, in 807 (Daidō 2). By imperial command from Emperor Saga, he carved the principal deity in 818 (Kōnin 9) and conducted memorial services for the Seven Buddhas. The temple became a training ground for monks and was later designated as an imperial prayer temple by Emperor Seiwa during the Jōgan era (857–877).

In 1098 (Shōtoku 2), the temple was destroyed by fire. A hunter named Yonari, who entered the area chasing deer, was said to have witnessed the manifestation of Kannon and rebuilt the temple out of devotion. During the late Heian period, Gansai Shōnin, considered the temple's restorer, created the existing statues of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and Bishamonten. The temple thrived under the patronage of Sasaki Tsunetaka (Kyōren) during the Kamakura period, who established a seven-hall complex. In 1311 (Ōchō 1), the Saionji family in Kyoto donated temple lands, and in 1363 (Jōji 2), the Ashikaga clan requested the chanting of the Lotus Sutra.

In 1577 (Tenshō 5), the Sengoku daimyō Chōsokabe Motochika visited Unpenji and shared his vision of uniting Shikoku with the temple's head priest.

== Highlights ==
The temple precincts feature several notable structures:
* Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== Cultural Properties ==
; Important Cultural Properties
* Wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon - Attributed to Keijin, carved during the late Heian period. Designated on August 9, 1911.
* Wooden statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten - Created by Keison in 1184 (Juei 3). Designated on October 31, 2018.

== Access ==
* Train: [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]] Dosan Line – 10.5 km from Minawa Station
* Ropeway: 10-minute walk from the mountaintop station
* Parking: Free parking available at the base station; fees a11ly near the temple precincts.

'''Unpenji''' (雲辺寺) is a {{ill|Shingon Omuro sect|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro sect|1=Omuro school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q420739}} temple located near the summit of {{ill|Unpenji Mountain|ja|雲辺寺山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unpenji Mountain|1=Mount Unpenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11660089}} (927 m above sea level) in [[:en:Miyoshi, Tokushima|Miyoshi, Tokushima]], formerly [[:en:Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District, Tokushima]], in Tokushima Prefecture, Japan. The temple is formally known as Kyogozan Senjuin (巨鼇山千手院) and is the 66th temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. The principal deity is {{ill|sahasrabhuja|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|fr|sahasrabhuja=sahasrabhuja|lt=Thousand-Armed Avalokiteshvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sahasrabhuja|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.

== Overview ==
Unpenji is the 66th temple on the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]], located in Tokushima Prefecture (formerly Awa Province), although it is treated as a temple of [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]] for the pilgrimage. It is known as the "Nehan Stage" (涅槃の道場), marking the start of the final section of the pilgrimage. Situated at an elevation of approximately 900 m near the main hall, it is the highest temple on the Shikoku Pilgrimage route and serves as a final checkpoint for pilgrims. The temple is renowned for its hydrangeas in summer, its autumn foliage, and its snow-covered landscapes in winter, made accessible by a ropeway.

== History ==
According to temple tradition, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi) established Unpenji in 789 (Enryaku 8) after ascending Unpenji Mountain to procure timber for the construction of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsūji]]. Deeming the site sacred, he constructed a temple and later conducted esoteric rites, including the secret abhiṣeka, in 807 (Daidō 2). By imperial command from Emperor Saga, he carved the principal deity in 818 (Kōnin 9) and conducted memorial services for the Seven Buddhas. The temple became a training ground for monks and was later designated as an imperial prayer temple by Emperor Seiwa during the Jōgan era (857–877).

In 1098 (Shōtoku 2), the temple was destroyed by fire. A hunter named Yonari, who entered the area chasing deer, was said to have witnessed the manifestation of Kannon and rebuilt the temple out of devotion. During the late Heian period, Gansai Shōnin, considered the temple's restorer, created the existing statues of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and Bishamonten. The temple thrived under the patronage of Sasaki Tsunetaka (Kyōren) during the Kamakura period, who established a seven-hall complex. In 1311 (Ōchō 1), the Saionji family in Kyoto donated temple lands, and in 1363 (Jōji 2), the Ashikaga clan requested the chanting of the Lotus Sutra.

In 1577 (Tenshō 5), the Sengoku daimyō Chōsokabe Motochika visited Unpenji and shared his vision of uniting Shikoku with the temple's head priest.

== Highlights ==
The temple precincts feature several notable structures:
* Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== Cultural Properties ==
; Important Cultural Properties
* Wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon - Attributed to Keijin, carved during the late Heian period. Designated on August 9, 1911.
* Wooden statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten - Created by Keison in 1184 (Juei 3). Designated on October 31, 2018.

== Access ==
* Train: [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]] Dosan Line – 10.5 km from Minawa Station
* Ropeway: 10-minute walk from the mountaintop station
* Parking: Free parking available at the base station; fees a11ly near the temple precincts.

'''Unpenji''' (雲辺寺) is a {{ill|Shingon Omuro sect|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro sect|1=Omuro school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q420739}} temple located near the summit of {{ill|Unpenji Mountain|ja|雲辺寺山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unpenji Mountain|1=Mount Unpenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11660089}} (927 m above sea level) in [[:en:Miyoshi, Tokushima|Miyoshi, Tokushima]], formerly [[:en:Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District, Tokushima]], in Tokushima Prefecture, Japan. The temple is formally known as Kyogozan Senjuin (巨鼇山千手院) and is the 66th temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. The principal deity is {{ill|sahasrabhuja|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|fr|sahasrabhuja=sahasrabhuja|lt=Thousand-Armed Avalokiteshvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sahasrabhuja|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.

== Overview ==
Unpenji is the 66th temple on the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]], located in Tokushima Prefecture (formerly Awa Province), although it is treated as a temple of [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]] for the pilgrimage. It is known as the "Nehan Stage" (涅槃の道場), marking the start of the final section of the pilgrimage. Situated at an elevation of approximately 900 m near the main hall, it is the highest temple on the Shikoku Pilgrimage route and serves as a final checkpoint for pilgrims. The temple is renowned for its hydrangeas in summer, its autumn foliage, and its snow-covered landscapes in winter, made accessible by a ropeway.

== History ==
According to temple tradition, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi) established Unpenji in 789 (Enryaku 8) after ascending Unpenji Mountain to procure timber for the construction of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsūji]]. Deeming the site sacred, he constructed a temple and later conducted esoteric rites, including the secret abhiṣeka, in 807 (Daidō 2). By imperial command from Emperor Saga, he carved the principal deity in 818 (Kōnin 9) and conducted memorial services for the Seven Buddhas. The temple became a training ground for monks and was later designated as an imperial prayer temple by Emperor Seiwa during the Jōgan era (857–877).

In 1098 (Shōtoku 2), the temple was destroyed by fire. A hunter named Yonari, who entered the area chasing deer, was said to have witnessed the manifestation of Kannon and rebuilt the temple out of devotion. During the late Heian period, Gansai Shōnin, considered the temple's restorer, created the existing statues of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and Bishamonten. The temple thrived under the patronage of Sasaki Tsunetaka (Kyōren) during the Kamakura period, who established a seven-hall complex. In 1311 (Ōchō 1), the Saionji family in Kyoto donated temple lands, and in 1363 (Jōji 2), the Ashikaga clan requested the chanting of the Lotus Sutra.

In 1577 (Tenshō 5), the Sengoku daimyō Chōsokabe Motochika visited Unpenji and shared his vision of uniting Shikoku with the temple's head priest.

== Highlights ==
The temple precincts feature several notable structures:
* Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== Cultural Properties ==
; Important Cultural Properties
* Wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon - Attributed to Keijin, carved during the late Heian period. Designated on August 9, 1911.
* Wooden statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten - Created by Keison in 1184 (Juei 3). Designated on October 31, 2018.

== Access ==
* Train: [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]] Dosan Line – 10.5 km from Minawa Station
* Ropeway: 10-minute walk from the mountaintop station
* Parking: Free parking available at the base station; fees a11ly near the temple precincts.

The principal deity is {{ill|sahasrabhuja|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|fr|sahasrabhuja=sahasrabhuja|lt=Thousand-Armed Avalokiteshvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sahasrabhuja|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.

== Overview ==
Unpenji is the 66th temple on the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]], located in Tokushima Prefecture (formerly Awa Province), although it is treated as a temple of [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]] for the pilgrimage. It is known as the "Nehan Stage" (涅槃の道場), marking the start of the final section of the pilgrimage. Situated at an elevation of approximately 900 m near the main hall, it is the highest temple on the Shikoku Pilgrimage route and serves as a final checkpoint for pilgrims. The temple is renowned for its hydrangeas in summer, its autumn foliage, and its snow-covered landscapes in winter, made accessible by a ropeway.

== History ==
According to temple tradition, [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi) established Unpenji in 789 (Enryaku 8) after ascending Unpenji Mountain to procure timber for the construction of [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsūji]]. Deeming the site sacred, he constructed a temple and later conducted esoteric rites, including the secret abhiṣeka, in 807 (Daidō 2). By imperial command from Emperor Saga, he carved the principal deity in 818 (Kōnin 9) and conducted memorial services for the Seven Buddhas. The temple became a training ground for monks and was later designated as an imperial prayer temple by Emperor Seiwa during the Jōgan era (857–877).

In 1098 (Shōtoku 2), the temple was destroyed by fire. A hunter named Yonari, who entered the area chasing deer, was said to have witnessed the manifestation of Kannon and rebuilt the temple out of devotion. During the late Heian period, Gansai Shōnin, considered the temple's restorer, created the existing statues of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and Bishamonten. The temple thrived under the patronage of Sasaki Tsunetaka (Kyōren) during the Kamakura period, who established a seven-hall complex. In 1311 (Ōchō 1), the Saionji family in Kyoto donated temple lands, and in 1363 (Jōji 2), the Ashikaga clan requested the chanting of the Lotus Sutra.

In 1577 (Tenshō 5), the Sengoku daimyō Chōsokabe Motochika visited Unpenji and shared his vision of uniting Shikoku with the temple's head priest.

== Highlights ==
The temple precincts feature several notable structures:
* Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== Cultural Properties ==
; Important Cultural Properties
* Wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon - Attributed to Keijin, carved during the late Heian period. Designated on August 9, 1911.
* Wooden statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten - Created by Keison in 1184 (Juei 3). Designated on October 31, 2018.

== Access ==
* Train: [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]] Dosan Line – 10.5 km from Minawa Station
* Ropeway: 10-minute walk from the mountaintop station
* Parking: Free parking available at the base station; fees a11ly near the temple precincts.

The temple is renowned for its hydrangeas in summer, its autumn foliage, and its snow-covered landscapes in winter, made accessible by a ropeway.

According to temple tradition, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi) established Unpenji in 789 (Enryaku 8) after ascending Unpenji Mountain to procure timber for the construction of {{ill|Zentsū-ji|en|Zentsū-ji|WD=Q787035|lt=Zentsūji}}. By imperial command from Emperor Saga, he carved the principal deity in 818 (Kōnin 9) and conducted memorial services for the Seven Buddhas. The temple became a training ground for monks and was later designated as an imperial prayer temple by Emperor Seiwa during the Jōgan era (857–877).

In 1098 (Shōtoku 2), the temple was destroyed by fire. A hunter named Yonari, who entered the area chasing deer, was said to have witnessed the manifestation of Kannon and rebuilt the temple out of devotion. During the late Heian period, Gansai Shōnin, considered the temple's restorer, created the existing statues of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and Bishamonten. * Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== History ==
According to temple tradition, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi) established Unpenji in 789 (Enryaku 8) after ascending Unpenji Mountain to procure timber for the construction of {{ill|Zentsū-ji|en|Zentsū-ji|WD=Q787035|lt=Zentsūji}}. Deeming the site sacred, he constructed a temple and later conducted esoteric rites, including the secret abhiṣeka, in 807 (Daidō 2). By imperial command from Emperor Saga, he carved the principal deity in 818 (Kōnin 9) and conducted memorial services for the Seven Buddhas. The temple became a training ground for monks and was later designated as an imperial prayer temple by Emperor Seiwa during the Jōgan era (857–877).

In 1098 (Shōtoku 2), the temple was destroyed by fire. A hunter named Yonari, who entered the area chasing deer, was said to have witnessed the manifestation of Kannon and rebuilt the temple out of devotion. During the late Heian period, Gansai Shōnin, considered the temple's restorer, created the existing statues of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and Bishamonten. The temple thrived under the patronage of Sasaki Tsunetaka (Kyōren) during the Kamakura period, who established a seven-hall complex. In 1311 (Ōchō 1), the Saionji family in Kyoto donated temple lands, and in 1363 (Jōji 2), the Ashikaga clan requested the chanting of the Lotus Sutra.

In 1577 (Tenshō 5), the Sengoku daimyō Chōsokabe Motochika visited Unpenji and shared his vision of uniting Shikoku with the temple's head priest.

== Highlights ==
The temple precincts feature several notable structures:
* Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== Cultural Properties ==
; Important Cultural Properties
* Wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon - Attributed to Keijin, carved during the late Heian period. Designated on August 9, 1911.
* Wooden statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten - Created by Keison in 1184 (Juei 3). Designated on October 31, 2018.

== Access ==
* Train: {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|en|Shikoku Railway Company|WD=Q496531|lt=JR Shikoku}} Dosan Line – 10.5 km from Minawa Station
* Ropeway: 10-minute walk from the mountaintop station
* Parking: Free parking available at the base station; fees a11ly near the temple precincts.

'''Unpenji''' (雲辺寺) is a {{ill|Shingon Omuro sect|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro sect|1=Omuro school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q420739}} temple located near the summit of {{ill|Unpenji Mountain|ja|雲辺寺山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unpenji Mountain|1=Mount Unpenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11660089}} (927 m above sea level) in {{ill|Miyoshi, Tokushima|en|Miyoshi, Tokushima|WD=Q990371|lt=Miyoshi, Tokushima}}, formerly {{ill|Miyoshi District, Tokushima|en|Miyoshi District, Tokushima|WD=Q1156031|lt=Miyoshi District, Tokushima}}, in Tokushima Prefecture, Japan. The temple is formally known as Kyogozan Senjuin (巨鼇山千手院) and is the 66th temple of the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}. The principal deity is {{ill|sahasrabhuja|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|fr|sahasrabhuja=sahasrabhuja|lt=Thousand-Armed Avalokiteshvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sahasrabhuja|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.

== Overview ==
Unpenji is the 66th temple on the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}, located in Tokushima Prefecture (formerly Awa Province), although it is treated as a temple of {{ill|Sanuki Province|en|Sanuki Province|WD=Q929682|lt=Sanuki Province}} for the pilgrimage. It is known as the "Nehan Stage" (涅槃の道場), marking the start of the final section of the pilgrimage. Situated at an elevation of approximately 900 m near the main hall, it is the highest temple on the Shikoku Pilgrimage route and serves as a final checkpoint for pilgrims. The temple is renowned for its hydrangeas in summer, its autumn foliage, and its snow-covered landscapes in winter, made accessible by a ropeway.

== History ==
According to temple tradition, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi) established Unpenji in 789 (Enryaku 8) after ascending Unpenji Mountain to procure timber for the construction of {{ill|Zentsū-ji|en|Zentsū-ji|WD=Q787035|lt=Zentsūji}}. Deeming the site sacred, he constructed a temple and later conducted esoteric rites, including the secret abhiṣeka, in 807 (Daidō 2). By imperial command from Emperor Saga, he carved the principal deity in 818 (Kōnin 9) and conducted memorial services for the Seven Buddhas. The temple became a training ground for monks and was later designated as an imperial prayer temple by Emperor Seiwa during the Jōgan era (857–877).

In 1098 (Shōtoku 2), the temple was destroyed by fire. A hunter named Yonari, who entered the area chasing deer, was said to have witnessed the manifestation of Kannon and rebuilt the temple out of devotion. During the late Heian period, Gansai Shōnin, considered the temple's restorer, created the existing statues of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and Bishamonten. The temple thrived under the patronage of Sasaki Tsunetaka (Kyōren) during the Kamakura period, who established a seven-hall complex. In 1311 (Ōchō 1), the Saionji family in Kyoto donated temple lands, and in 1363 (Jōji 2), the Ashikaga clan requested the chanting of the Lotus Sutra.

In 1577 (Tenshō 5), the Sengoku daimyō Chōsokabe Motochika visited Unpenji and shared his vision of uniting Shikoku with the temple's head priest.

== Highlights ==
The temple precincts feature several notable structures:
* Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== Cultural Properties ==
; Important Cultural Properties
* Wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon - Attributed to Keijin, carved during the late Heian period. Designated on August 9, 1911.
* Wooden statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten - Created by Keison in 1184 (Juei 3). Designated on October 31, 2018.

== Access ==
* Train: {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|en|Shikoku Railway Company|WD=Q496531|lt=JR Shikoku}} Dosan Line – 10.5 km from Minawa Station
* Ropeway: 10-minute walk from the mountaintop station
* Parking: Free parking available at the base station; fees a11ly near the temple precincts.

In 1311 (Ōchō 1), the Saionji family in Kyoto donated temple lands, and in 1363 (Jōji 2), the Ashikaga clan requested the chanting of the Lotus Sutra.

In 1577 (Tenshō 5), the Sengoku daimyō Chōsokabe Motochika visited Unpenji and shared his vision of uniting Shikoku with the temple's head priest.

== Highlights ==
The temple precincts feature several notable structures:
* Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== Cultural Properties ==
; Important Cultural Properties
* Wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon - Attributed to Keijin, carved during the late Heian period. Designated on August 9, 1911.
* Wooden statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten - Created by Keison in 1184 (Juei 3). Designated on October 31, 2018.

== Access ==
* Train: {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|en|Shikoku Railway Company|WD=Q496531|lt=JR Shikoku}} Dosan Line – 10.5 km from Minawa Station
* Ropeway: 10-minute walk from the mountaintop station
* Parking: Free parking available at the base station; fees a11ly near the temple precincts.

'''Unpenji''' (雲辺寺) is a {{ill|Shingon Omuro sect|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro sect|1=Omuro school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q420739}} temple located near the summit of {{ill|Unpenji Mountain|ja|雲辺寺山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unpenji Mountain|1=Mount Unpenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11660089}} (927 m above sea level) in {{ill|Miyoshi, Tokushima|en|Miyoshi, Tokushima|WD=Q990371|lt=Miyoshi, Tokushima}}, formerly {{ill|Miyoshi District, Tokushima|en|Miyoshi District, Tokushima|WD=Q1156031|lt=Miyoshi District, Tokushima}}, in Tokushima Prefecture, Japan. The temple is formally known as Kyogozan Senjuin (巨鼇山千手院) and is the 66th temple of the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}. The principal deity is {{ill|sahasrabhuja|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|fr|sahasrabhuja=sahasrabhuja|lt=Thousand-Armed Avalokiteshvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sahasrabhuja|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.

== Overview ==
Unpenji is the 66th temple on the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}, located in Tokushima Prefecture (formerly Awa Province), although it is treated as a temple of {{ill|Sanuki Province|en|Sanuki Province|WD=Q929682|lt=Sanuki Province}} for the pilgrimage. It is known as the "Nehan Stage" (涅槃の道場), marking the start of the final section of the pilgrimage. Situated at an elevation of approximately 900 m near the main hall, it is the highest temple on the Shikoku Pilgrimage route and serves as a final checkpoint for pilgrims. The temple is renowned for its hydrangeas in summer, its autumn foliage, and its snow-covered landscapes in winter, made accessible by a ropeway.

== History ==
According to temple tradition, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi) established Unpenji in 789 (Enryaku 8) after ascending Unpenji Mountain to procure timber for the construction of {{ill|Zentsū-ji|en|Zentsū-ji|WD=Q787035|lt=Zentsūji}}. Deeming the site sacred, he constructed a temple and later conducted esoteric rites, including the secret abhiṣeka, in 807 (Daidō 2). By imperial command from Emperor Saga, he carved the principal deity in 818 (Kōnin 9) and conducted memorial services for the Seven Buddhas. The temple became a training ground for monks and was later designated as an imperial prayer temple by Emperor Seiwa during the Jōgan era (857–877).

In 1098 (Shōtoku 2), the temple was destroyed by fire. A hunter named Yonari, who entered the area chasing deer, was said to have witnessed the manifestation of Kannon and rebuilt the temple out of devotion. During the late Heian period, Gansai Shōnin, considered the temple's restorer, created the existing statues of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and Bishamonten. The temple thrived under the patronage of Sasaki Tsunetaka (Kyōren) during the Kamakura period, who established a seven-hall complex. In 1311 (Ōchō 1), the Saionji family in Kyoto donated temple lands, and in 1363 (Jōji 2), the Ashikaga clan requested the chanting of the Lotus Sutra.

In 1577 (Tenshō 5), the Sengoku daimyō Chōsokabe Motochika visited Unpenji and shared his vision of uniting Shikoku with the temple's head priest.

== Highlights ==
The temple precincts feature several notable structures:
* Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== Cultural Properties ==
; Important Cultural Properties
* Wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon - Attributed to Keijin, carved during the late Heian period. Designated on August 9, 1911.
* Wooden statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten - Created by Keison in 1184 (Juei 3). Designated on October 31, 2018.

== Access ==
* Train: {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|en|Shikoku Railway Company|WD=Q496531|lt=JR Shikoku}} Dosan Line – 10.5 km from Minawa Station
* Ropeway: 10-minute walk from the mountaintop station
* Parking: Free parking available at the base station; fees a11ly near the temple precincts.

'''Unpenji''' (雲辺寺) is a {{ill|Shingon Omuro sect|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro sect|1=Shingon Omuro School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q420739}} temple located near the summit of {{ill|Unpenji Mountain|ja|雲辺寺山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unpenji Mountain|1=Mount Unpenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11660089}} (927 m above sea level) in {{ill|Miyoshi, Tokushima|en|Miyoshi, Tokushima|WD=Q990371|lt=Miyoshi, Tokushima|1=Miyoshi, Tokushima}}, formerly {{ill|Miyoshi District, Tokushima|en|Miyoshi District, Tokushima|WD=Q1156031|lt=Miyoshi District, Tokushima|1=Miyoshi District, Tokushima}}, in Tokushima Prefecture, Japan. The temple is formally known as Kyogozan Senjuin (巨鼇山千手院) and is the 66th temple of the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}. The principal deity is {{ill|sahasrabhuja|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|fr|sahasrabhuja=sahasrabhuja|lt=Thousand-Armed Avalokiteshvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sahasrabhuja|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.

== Overview ==
Unpenji is the 66th temple on the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}, located in Tokushima Prefecture (formerly Awa Province), although it is treated as a temple of {{ill|Sanuki Province|en|Sanuki Province|WD=Q929682|lt=Sanuki Province|1=Sanuki Province}} for the pilgrimage. It is known as the "Nehan Stage" (涅槃の道場), marking the start of the final section of the pilgrimage. Situated at an elevation of approximately 900 m near the main hall, it is the highest temple on the Shikoku Pilgrimage route and serves as a final checkpoint for pilgrims. The temple is renowned for its hydrangeas in summer, its autumn foliage, and its snow-covered landscapes in winter, made accessible by a ropeway.

== History ==
According to temple tradition, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi) established Unpenji in 789 (Enryaku 8) after ascending Unpenji Mountain to procure timber for the construction of {{ill|Zentsū-ji|en|Zentsū-ji|WD=Q787035|lt=Zentsūji|1=Zentsū-ji}}. Deeming the site sacred, he constructed a temple and later conducted esoteric rites, including the secret abhiṣeka, in 807 (Daidō 2). By imperial command from Emperor Saga, he carved the principal deity in 818 (Kōnin 9) and conducted memorial services for the Seven Buddhas. The temple became a training ground for monks and was later designated as an imperial prayer temple by Emperor Seiwa during the Jōgan era (857–877).

In 1098 (Shōtoku 2), the temple was destroyed by fire. A hunter named Yonari, who entered the area chasing deer, was said to have witnessed the manifestation of Kannon and rebuilt the temple out of devotion. During the late Heian period, Gansai Shōnin, considered the temple's restorer, created the existing statues of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and Bishamonten. The temple thrived under the patronage of Sasaki Tsunetaka (Kyōren) during the Kamakura period, who established a seven-hall complex. In 1311 (Ōchō 1), the Saionji family in Kyoto donated temple lands, and in 1363 (Jōji 2), the Ashikaga clan requested the chanting of the Lotus Sutra.

In 1577 (Tenshō 5), the Sengoku daimyō Chōsokabe Motochika visited Unpenji and shared his vision of uniting Shikoku with the temple's head priest.

== Highlights ==
The temple precincts feature several notable structures:
* Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== Cultural Properties ==
; Important Cultural Properties
* Wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon - Attributed to Keijin, carved during the late Heian period. Designated on August 9, 1911.
* Wooden statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten - Created by Keison in 1184 (Juei 3). Designated on October 31, 2018.

== Access ==
* Train: {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|en|Shikoku Railway Company|WD=Q496531|lt=JR Shikoku|1=Shikoku Railway Company}} Dosan Line – 10.5 km from Minawa Station
* Ropeway: 10-minute walk from the mountaintop station
* Parking: Free parking available at the base station; fees a11ly near the temple precincts.

'''Unpenji''' (雲辺寺) is a {{ill|Shingon Omuro sect|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro sect|1=Shingon Omuro School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q420739}} temple located near the summit of {{ill|Unpenji Mountain|ja|雲辺寺山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unpenji Mountain|1=Mount Unpenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11660089}} (927 m above sea level) in {{ill|Miyoshi, Tokushima|en|Miyoshi, Tokushima|WD=Q990371|lt=Miyoshi, Tokushima|1=Miyoshi, Tokushima}}, formerly {{ill|Miyoshi District, Tokushima|en|Miyoshi District, Tokushima|WD=Q1156031|lt=Miyoshi District, Tokushima|1=Miyoshi District, Tokushima}}, in Tokushima Prefecture, Japan. The temple is formally known as Kyogozan Senjuin (巨鼇山千手院) and is the 66th temple of the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}. The principal deity is {{ill|sahasrabhuja|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|fr|sahasrabhuja=sahasrabhuja|lt=Thousand-Armed Avalokiteshvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sahasrabhuja|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.

== Overview ==
Unpenji is the 66th temple on the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}, located in Tokushima Prefecture (formerly Awa Province), although it is treated as a temple of {{ill|Sanuki Province|en|Sanuki Province|WD=Q929682|lt=Sanuki Province|1=Sanuki Province}} for the pilgrimage. It is known as the "Nehan Stage" (涅槃の道場), marking the start of the final section of the pilgrimage. Situated at an elevation of approximately 900 m near the main hall, it is the highest temple on the Shikoku Pilgrimage route and serves as a final checkpoint for pilgrims. The temple is renowned for its hydrangeas in summer, its autumn foliage, and its snow-covered landscapes in winter, made accessible by a ropeway.

== History ==
According to temple tradition, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}} (Kōbō Daishi) established Unpenji in 789 (Enryaku 8) after ascending Unpenji Mountain to procure timber for the construction of {{ill|Zentsū-ji|en|Zentsū-ji|WD=Q787035|lt=Zentsūji|1=Zentsū-ji}}. Deeming the site sacred, he constructed a temple and later conducted esoteric rites, including the secret abhiṣeka, in 807 (Daidō 2). By imperial command from Emperor Saga, he carved the principal deity in 818 (Kōnin 9) and conducted memorial services for the Seven Buddhas. The temple became a training ground for monks and was later designated as an imperial prayer temple by Emperor Seiwa during the Jōgan era (857–877).

In 1098 (Shōtoku 2), the temple was destroyed by fire. A hunter named Yonari, who entered the area chasing deer, was said to have witnessed the manifestation of Kannon and rebuilt the temple out of devotion. During the late Heian period, Gansai Shōnin, considered the temple's restorer, created the existing statues of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and Bishamonten. The temple thrived under the patronage of Sasaki Tsunetaka (Kyōren) during the Kamakura period, who established a seven-hall complex. In 1311 (Ōchō 1), the Saionji family in Kyoto donated temple lands, and in 1363 (Jōji 2), the Ashikaga clan requested the chanting of the Lotus Sutra.

In 1577 (Tenshō 5), the Sengoku daimyō Chōsokabe Motochika visited Unpenji and shared his vision of uniting Shikoku with the temple's head priest.

== Highlights ==
The temple precincts feature several notable structures:
* Main Hall: Completed in 2009, housing a seated stone statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon and flanked by statues of the Black Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten.
* Treasure Hall: Contains the temple’s cultural assets, including the wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon (Important Cultural Property) and a collection of Buddhist statues from the Heian and Kamakura periods.
* Bishamonten Observatory: Offers a 360-degree panoramic view at an elevation of 927 m.

== Cultural Properties ==
; Important Cultural Properties
* Wooden seated statue of the Thousand-Armed Kannon - Attributed to Keijin, carved during the late Heian period. Designated on August 9, 1911.
* Wooden statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten - Created by Keison in 1184 (Juei 3). Designated on October 31, 2018.

== Access ==
* Train: {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|en|Shikoku Railway Company|WD=Q496531|lt=JR Shikoku|1=Shikoku Railway Company}} Dosan Line – 10.5 km from Minawa Station
* Ropeway: 10-minute walk from the mountaintop station
* Parking: Free parking available at the base station; fees a11ly near the temple precincts.
